preamble: The voiced palatal lateral approximant /ʎ/ frequently undergoes vocalization, transforming into a voiced palatal approximant /j/. This palatal approximant is phonetically the non-syllabic equivalent of the close front unrounded vowel /i/, sharing its place and manner of articulation (high front unrounded). This process, often termed delateralization or gliding, represents a shift from a consonantal lateral articulation to a vocalic or semivocalic one.
phoneticEffects: Vocalization, Lenition, Delateralization, Gliding, Approximantization, Vowelization

examples.note: The historical palatal lateral /ʎ/ in words like 'lleno' (full) merged with the palatal approximant /j/ due to yeísmo.
The palatal lateral /ʎ/ in 'ella' (she) shifted to the palatal approximant /j/.
From Latin 'filia' to Old Occitan /fiʎɔ/, which then reduced to /fijɔ/ (daughter) since the 17th century.
From Latin 'palea' to Occitan /pajɔ/ (straw), showing the reduction of /ʎ/ to /j/.

sources: García-Bellido, J. M. (2025). Variation and Phonological Change: The Case of Yeísmo in Spanish. Academia.edu.
García-Bellido, J. M. (2021). Variation and phonological change. The case of "yeísmo" in Spanish. ResearchGate.
Ledgeway, A. (2017). Southern Gallo-Romance: Occitan and Gascon. Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Linguistics.
